actions.thought: I am on the button with Q6 offsuit. This is a weak starting hand with poor playability, especially at the start of a 150-hand tournament. While I am in a late position, Q6o does not warrant an open-raise or a call against the big blind. It is best to fold and wait for a more advantageous hand to enter the pot.

I am in the Small Blind with Ace-Queen offsuit, a premium starting hand. The Button has folded, leaving it heads-up between me and the Big Blind. I want to raise to build the pot for value and deny the Big Blind a free look at the flop with marginal hands. A standard raise size from the Small Blind is 3x the Big Blind, so I will raise to 300.

I am holding 7s 2s in the Big Blind. While the cards are suited, 7-2 is mathematically the weakest starting hand in Texas Hold'em. The Small Blind has raised to 3x the big blind. Although I would have post-flop position, defending with such a weak hand is a negative expected value (-EV) play in the long run. I will fold and wait for a better spot.

I am first to act on the Button in a 3-handed game with KQo. This is a premium starting hand in this format. I will raise to 3x the big blind (300) to build the pot, take control of the hand, and likely fold out weaker hands in the blinds while setting up profitable post-flop play.

Ace-Ten offsuit is a strong hand in a three-handed game, particularly when facing a standard button open. While calling is an option, three-betting allows me to take control of the pot, realize equity more effectively, and potentially fold out the big blind. I will raise to 1000 to apply pressure to the button and capitalize on the strength of my hand.

I am in the Big Blind with pocket threes, facing a 3-bet squeeze from the Small Blind after the Button opened. Calling a 3-bet out of position against two active ranges is mathematically unfavorable with a small pair. The set-mining odds are insufficient given the 3-bet sizing, and I risk being dominated or facing a 4-bet from the Button. Folding preserves my stack early in the tournament for better opportunities.

I opened KQo from the Button, which is standard in a 3-handed game. However, facing a large 3-bet to 10x the big blind from the Small Blind, KQo is often dominated by their value 3-betting range such as AK, AQ, and big pocket pairs. Despite having position post-flop, calling a 3-bet with an easily dominated offsuit broadway hand is unprofitable in the long run. Folding is the safest and most disciplined play to preserve my stack early in the tournament.

I have Ace-King of hearts on the button, which is a premium hand. I want to raise for value and to gain initiative in the hand. A standard 3x open to 300 is appropriate here to build the pot while in position.

I hold a medium pocket pair, 7d 7s, which is a strong starting hand. Since I am in the small blind facing an open raise from the button, I want to take the initiative and prevent the big blind from coming along cheaply. A 3-bet to 950 is an appropriate sizing to either force a fold or play a bloated pot in position if they call.

Facing a button open and a small blind 3-bet, Ace-Queen offsuit is a strong hand but plays poorly as a cold call. If we call, we risk a 4-bet from the button, and against the small blind's 3-betting range, we are frequently dominated by AK or premium pairs. While a cold 4-bet bluff is an option, it is unnecessarily high variance this early in the tournament. Folding is the disciplined and optimal play.

I have AKs on the button, which is a premium starting hand. After my initial raise, Flashlight (SB) 3-bet to 950. AKs is too strong to simply fold and plays excellently in a 4-bet pot, especially when in position. I will 4-bet to 2300 to build the pot for value and potentially take the hand down pre-flop. This sizing is standard and puts pressure on Flashlight's 3-betting range while leaving me room to navigate if they shove or call.

Opponent has 4-bet my 3-bet. Pocket 7s are vulnerable to a tight 4-bet range, which typically consists of high pairs (JJ+) and big aces (AK). Being out of position and facing heavy aggression this early in the tournament, continuing would require hitting a set, which is statistically unlikely. Folding minimizes further loss and preserves my stack for better opportunities.

Queen-Six offsuit is a marginal starting hand that lacks playability and strength. In a three-handed game with 90 big blinds, opening this hand from the button is negative expected value. It is better to remain disciplined and fold, waiting for a stronger hand to enter the pot.

I am in the Small Blind with an extremely weak hand (3d 8s). Flashlight folded on the Button. Flash is in the Big Blind and has shown significant aggression in previous hands. Given my poor cards and positional disadvantage post-flop against an aggressive opponent, the optimal and safest play is to fold.
6s 2s is a very weak hand, easily dominated and not worth playing even from the button. I will fold and wait for better starting hands.

I am holding 4c 3s in the Small Blind. This is one of the weakest starting hands in Texas Hold'em. With the button folding, it is just me and the Big Blind. While I could attempt a steal, 43o has very little playability post-flop and I would be out of position. Given my healthy stack and the early stage of the tournament, folding is the most disciplined and standard play here to avoid unnecessary losses with a trash hand.

I am on the button with Jack-Ten offsuit in a 3-handed game. This is a standard opening hand. I have a healthy stack and have shown aggression in previous hands, which should make my button raises respected. I will raise to 300 (3x the big blind) to take control of the pot and leverage my positional advantage.

I am in the small blind with Ace-Five offsuit. This is a playable hand to defend from the small blind against a button open, as an Ace provides decent board coverage. 3-betting out of position against the button can be high-variance this early in the tournament, so I will call to see a flop and evaluate my position, taking advantage of the pot odds.

K8s is a marginal suited hand. Facing a 3x open from the Button and a flat call from the Small Blind, we will be playing out of position in a multiway pot. When we pair our King, we face severe reverse implied odds against better kickers that are prevalent in their ranges. Folding is the safer, higher EV play to avoid marginal spots out of position.

The flop of King-Jack-Seven is dry and does not connect with my Ace-Five offsuit. Being out of position with no pair and no meaningful draw, checking is the optimal play to maintain pot control and re-evaluate on the turn.

I have middle pair with a Ten kicker on a King-high board after raising pre-flop. My opponent checked to me, which often indicates weakness or a trapping hand. In a 3-handed game, middle pair is a solid hand that benefits from a continuation bet to extract value from weaker pairs, straight draws (like QT or T9), or to simply take down the pot. I will bet 350, which is half the pot, to maintain aggression and see how my opponent reacts.

The board of King-Jack-Seven is very favorable for the pre-flop aggressor's opening range. My Ace-high hand has no significant equity here; I lack a pair, straight draws, and flush draws. Calling this bet would be a speculative play with very little chance of winning the pot if the opponent has even a marginal piece of this board. Given the early stage of the tournament, folding is the disciplined choice to preserve my stack for a more advantageous situation.

With JTo on the button in a three-handed game, this is a standard opening range hand. Position is highly valuable, and I have enough equity to contest the blinds and take initiative. A raise to 250 (2.5x BB) is a standard sizing to put pressure on the blinds and potentially win the pot pre-flop.

My hand is Jack-Two offsuit, which is one of the weakest starting hands in Texas Hold'em. Facing a raise from the button and being out of position in the small blind, there is absolutely no reason to play this hand. The correct play is an easy fold to conserve my stack for better spots.

I am in the Big Blind with 4d 5s. Flashlight has raised to 250 from the Button, and the Small Blind has folded. I need to call 150 more into a pot of 400. While the odds are roughly 2.6:1, 4-5 offsuit is a very weak hand to play out of position against a Button range. Given the early stage of the tournament and my current chip lead, I prefer to fold this junk hand rather than enter a pot out of position where I will likely have to fold to post-flop aggression or miss the board entirely.

With AJo on the Button in a 3-handed game, this is a premium hand that I should definitely open. I will raise to 3x the big blind (300 chips) to apply pressure to the blinds, aiming to steal the pot pre-flop or play heads-up in position.

I am in the Small Blind with 7c2c, which is a very weak hand. The Button (Pro) has raised to 300. Although the cards are suited, 7-2 is mathematically one of the worst hands in Hold'em. Calling out of position against a raise from the Button is generally a losing play, and 3-betting as a bluff with this specific hand is too risky given the stack sizes and the early stage of the match. I will fold and wait for a better spot.

I am in the Big Blind with King-Queen offsuit, which is a strong holding in a 3-handed game. The pot offers me favorable odds to call (200 to win 450 total), and since the Small Blind has folded, I can see a flop in position relative to the raiser closing the pre-flop action. 3-betting is an option, but calling is a solid, low-variance play that allows me to realize my equity against the Button's opening range.

I am in the Big Blind with King-Queen offsuit on a board of 4d-Jc-3d. The pre-flop aggressor has checked to me, which often signals a lack of a strong connected piece, though they could be trapping. With only overcards and a backdoor flush draw, I am out of position and have no reason to bloat the pot. Checking allows me to realize my equity for free and keep the pot manageable.

I have top pair, top kicker on a somewhat draw-heavy board with flush and straight draws present. Flashlight checked to me. I should make a continuation bet to extract value from weaker Jacks, pocket pairs, and draws, while also denying free equity. A bet around half to two-thirds of the pot is standard here.

I am in the Big Blind with King-high and a backdoor flush draw on a relatively dry board of 4d-Jc-3d. My opponent (the button) has made a standard continuation bet. While I am currently behind any made pair, my overcards provide significant equity, and I am getting reasonable pot odds (350 to win 1000 total) to continue. Folding is too tight, and raising would be a unnecessary bluff. Calling allows me to realize my equity and evaluate the turn card.

I am in the Big Blind with top pair (Queens with King kicker) on a board that has become slightly more connected with the Queen of Spades on the turn. As the out-of-position player, checking is the most effective way to control the size of the pot and induce bluffs from the opponent's range, especially since there are potential diamond draws and straight draws present. Leading out into the pre-flop aggressor is generally suboptimal in this spot, as it forces out hands I beat while bloating the pot against hands that have me crushed (like sets or better).

The turn card is a Queen, which is an overcard to my pair of Jacks. I now have second pair, top kicker. While my hand still has decent showdown value, betting here is risky as it will likely only be called by better hands (like Qx, two pair, or sets) and will fold out worse hands (like weaker pocket pairs or floats). Checking behind allows me to control the pot size, keep Flashlight's draws in his range, avoid getting check-raised, and realize my equity going to the river.

I am in the Big Blind with a pair of Queens (King kicker). The board is 4d-Jc-3d-Qs-3h. My opponent checked the turn, indicating they likely have a marginal hand or are giving up on the pot. Betting here serves little purpose as I will likely only be called by hands that beat me (like a Jack, a set, or a better pair). By checking, I allow the hand to go to showdown, minimizing the risk of losing more chips if I am behind while still having a chance to win if the opponent is also holding a weak hand.

The river is a 3, pairing the bottom card on the board. Flashlight checks again. With second pair (Jacks) and an Ace kicker, I have solid showdown value, but very limited value-betting prospects. Flashlight's range consists of missed draws (which will fold), medium pocket pairs or weak Jacks (which might fold to a bet), and Queens or slow-played Threes (which beat me and will call or raise). Betting here is too thin and risks losing more chips to better hands. Checking behind allows me to safely reach showdown.

I am on the button with 4s 3d, which is an extremely weak hand. While position is valuable in a 3-handed game, 43o is at the bottom of the range and does not play well post-flop or against resistance. Given that Pro in the big blind has shown some aggression in previous hands, attempting a steal here is not worth the risk. I will fold and wait for a better starting hand.

With 4d 6s, I am holding a very weak hand in the small blind. While I have already invested 50 chips, completing the small blind against the big blind with such a marginal holding is generally negative expected value. It is more prudent to fold and preserve my stack for a more favorable situation.

I am on the button with 5d-4d, which is a playable suited connector. Being in position, I should raise to take the initiative and potentially win the blinds, or at least see a flop with the betting lead. A raise to 300 is a standard size to build the pot and exert pressure on the blinds.

Holding 9c5c in the Small Blind facing a 3x raise from the Button is a clear fold. The hand is too weak to call, as playing suited two-gappers out of position against a pre-flop aggressor is unprofitable. A 3-bet bluff is also unnecessary with such a weak hand when I can choose better suited connectors or Broadway cards.

I am in the Big Blind with 9h 3h. Flashlight has raised to 300 from the button, and Pro has folded. I need to call 200 more to see a flop in a pot that would be 650. While 93s has some flush potential, it is a very weak hand to play out of position against a standard button opening range. Given the stack depths and the early stage of the tournament, I will play disciplined and fold this marginal hand.

With Ten-Eight offsuit on the button, my hand is too weak to justify a standard open-raise, even in a 3-handed game. Opening here would put me in difficult post-flop situations against the blinds with a marginal holding. The most profitable play is to fold and preserve my stack for better opportunities.

With the Button folding, I am in the Small Blind with Q5s. In a 3-handed game, this hand is strong enough to open-raise. Raising to 3x (300 total) puts pressure on the Big Blind and allows me to take the initiative. Q5s has decent playability post-flop if called, with both high-card and flush potential.

I am in the Big Blind with King-Jack offsuit against a Small Blind raise. KJo is a strong holding in this position, and folding is too tight given the circumstances. While 3-betting is an option, calling allows me to realize my equity and play the hand in position against the Small Blind, keeping the pot manageable and avoiding bloating it against a potentially aggressive opponent.

I was the pre-flop aggressor and the flop of 3c-6s-3d is very dry, making it unlikely to have significantly improved the Big Blind's calling range. A continuation bet is standard here to maintain initiative and capitalize on the board texture. A sizing of 250 into the 600 pot (approx. 40%) is enough to fold out non-paired hands and test the opponent's strength without overcommitting with Queen-high.

The board is extremely dry (3-3-6) and unlikely to have connected well with the opponent's pre-flop raising range. My King-high is currently a bluff-catcher, but with the bet size being small (250 into 600), I am getting decent pot odds to continue. I have two overcards that provide some residual equity, and calling allows me to evaluate the turn. Raising would be counterproductive as it turns my hand into a bluff, forcing out hands I am currently beating while only getting called by better.

The turn 4d is an excellent card for my hand, giving me both an open-ended straight draw (3-4-5-6) and a flush draw. With 15 outs to a likely best hand (9 diamonds + 8 straight cards - 2 overlapping diamonds), I have significant equity. I will continue with a second barrel as a semi-bluff. This maintains my table image as the aggressor and puts maximum pressure on Flashlight's marginal holdings like mid-pairs or a 6, while I still have a high probability of winning the pot on the river if called.

The board is paired with 3-3, which significantly devalues my hand and reduces the likelihood of my King-high being ahead. My opponent has shown continued aggression from the small blind, betting into the turn. Given that my hand is effectively a bluff-catcher against a range that likely contains at least a 3, a 6, or a pocket pair, calling is not mathematically sound. Folding allows me to preserve my stack of 9,100 for a more favorable situation later in the tournament.

I am on the button with Q6o in a 3-handed game. While Q6o is a marginal hand, the button is the most advantageous position and it is standard to open a wide range here (roughly 40-50% of hands). Raising to 300 puts pressure on the blinds (Flashlight and Pro) and allows me to win the pot immediately or play a pot in position if called. My stack is currently the largest, allowing me to absorb the loss if I have to fold to a 3-bet.

Jack-three offsuit is an extremely weak starting hand with very low equity, especially out of position from the Small Blind against a standard Button opening range. There is no strategic justification to defend this hand in this spot, so I will fold and conserve my stack for a better opportunity.
Holding 93o in the big blind facing a button raise is an easy fold. The hand has very little post-flop playability, no flush potential, and terrible straight potential.

Holding King-Ten (Kd, Tc) on the button is a strong position to open the action in a 3-handed game. Opening to 2.5x the big blind (250 chips) is a standard strategy to steal the blinds and maintain initiative, leveraging my positional advantage.

I am in the Small Blind with 9h 5c, which is a very weak unsuited hand. Flashlight raised from the Button to 250. This is an easy fold, as the hand has very little playability or equity against a raise.

I am in the Big Blind with 82s in a 3-handed game. The Button (Flashlight) has opened for a standard 2.5x raise. While suited hands generally have better playability and 3-max ranges are much wider than full-ring, 8-high with a 2 kicker is extremely weak. Defending this hand out of position (OOP) against the chip leader's closest rival (in terms of position) puts me in a spot where I will likely under-realize my equity. Given I currently hold the chip lead, I will opt for a disciplined fold rather than defending with the bottom of my suited range and playing a potentially difficult pot OOP.
I have 85 offsuit on the button, which is a very weak hand. The most profitable play over the long run with unplayable junk like this is simply to fold and wait for better starting hands.

I am in the Small Blind with King-Trey offsuit. The Button has folded, making this a heads-up situation against the Big Blind (Flashlight). K3o is strong enough to attempt a steal from this position, especially since Flashlight has shown a tendency to fold to pre-flop and post-flop aggression in previous hands. I will raise to 300 to put pressure on the Big Blind and potentially take down the pot immediately.

7-4 offsuit is an extremely weak starting hand with very little playability. Even given the pot odds, calling a raise from the small blind with such a dominated hand is not mathematically sound. It is better to conserve chips for a more advantageous situation.

I am on the button with pocket Queens, a premier value hand. I will open with a standard 3x raise to build the pot and capitalize on my position. This sizing is consistent with my previous opening raises.

K6o is a weak hand to defend from the small blind against a button open. Being out of position for the remainder of the hand makes it difficult to realize equity effectively. Given that there is still a player behind in the big blind and there are plenty of hands remaining in the tournament, the most disciplined play is to fold and wait for a better opportunity.
Holding 9c 2s in the Big Blind facing a 3x raise from the Button. This hand is incredibly weak with virtually no playability or equity, making it a trivial fold despite getting pot odds.

Jack-nine offsuit is a weak starting hand in a three-handed game. While I am on the button, both opponents have shown significant aggression in previous hands. Folding is the most disciplined and profitable play here to preserve my stack for a more advantageous situation.

With A4o in the small blind and the button folded, raising is the best option to try and steal the big blind. If called, we still have an ace blocker and some equity, but taking down the pot pre-flop is the primary goal.

I am in the Big Blind with King-Eight offsuit. The Small Blind (Pro) has raised to 300. With 200 more to call into a pot that will be 600, I am getting 3:1 odds. King-high hands are generally strong enough to defend in this spot, especially since I will have position for the rest of the hand. I'll call to see a flop and evaluate based on the board texture.

On a dry, paired flop of T-7-T, as the pre-flop aggressor I have a range advantage that includes overpairs and strong Tx hands. A small continuation bet (about 1/3 pot) is standard to fold out my opponent's missed overcards and floats, taking down the pot cheaply with Ace high, which is otherwise difficult to play out of position if I check and face a bet.

I have King-high on a paired board of Tc-7d-Th. The Small Blind (Pro) has led out for 200 into a 600 pot (a 1/3 pot bet). I am getting 4:1 odds to call. Given the paired board and Pro's wide range from the Small Blind, King-high still has reasonable equity and potential showdown value. I will call the bet to see a turn and evaluate based on further action and board development.

The turn double pairs the board (T-T-7-7), which actually counterfeits any pocket pairs 22-66 my opponent might hold, making my Ace-high play as T-T-7-7-A, the best possible kicker to the board. If I bet, I only get called by better hands (a Ten, a Seven, or pocket pairs 88+). Checking allows me to get to showdown cheaply, pot control, and keep my opponent's missed broadway hands in the pot, which I currently beat.

The board has double-paired with Tens and Sevens (Tc-7d-Th-7h). I am holding King-high. My opponent, Pro, who was the pre-flop aggressor and bet the flop, has now checked on the turn. While their check might indicate weakness, King-high has some showdown value against their air hands or weaker draws. Betting here is unlikely to fold out better hands like Ace-high or pocket pairs, and I definitely won't get called by worse. Therefore, checking back to see a free river and potentially get to showdown is the most prudent play.

The river brings a 4, leaving the board double-paired with Tens and Sevens. My Ad4s gives me a final hand of T-T-7-7-A. Betting has no value here because worse hands (like King-high or counterfeited small pairs) will fold, and better hands (any Ten, Seven, or 88+ pocket pair) will call. I also chop with any other Ace-high. Checking is the most solid play to get to a cheap showdown, realize our equity, and potentially induce a bluff from missed draws.

The board is double-paired with Tens and Sevens (Tc-7d-Th-7h-4d). My hand is effectively two pair, Tens and Sevens, with a King kicker. Pro, the pre-flop aggressor who bet the flop, has checked the turn and river. This suggests they likely don't have a Ten or a Seven. My King kicker provides significant showdown value against Pro's potential bluffs, missed draws, or lower high cards (like Queen-high or Jack-high). There is no value in betting here, as better hands like Ace-high won't fold and worse hands are unlikely to call. Checking back is the standard and most profitable play.

On the button in a 3-handed game, K3 suited is well within standard open-raising ranges. It has good blocker value with the King and can make flushes or top pair, giving it decent playability if we get called. I'll make a standard 3x raise to 300 to steal the blinds or take the initiative into the flop.

I am holding 3-2 offsuit, which is one of the weakest hands in poker. Pro raised from the button, and I am in the small blind. Calling here would put me out of position with a hand that has very little equity or playability. Folding is the only sensible move in this spot.

QJo is a standard and strong hand to defend the big blind against a button open. With 200 to call into a pot that will be 450, the price is attractive enough to see a flop despite being out of position.

The flop is 6s-Th-6h, and I am out of position against the button pre-flop raiser. My hand (QJ) consists of two overcards without a pair. Checking is the standard approach out of position against the aggressor, as it allows me to evaluate their action and prevents me from bloat the pot when I am likely behind.

We flopped a strong King-high flush draw on a paired board. Since we have the betting initiative, a continuation bet is ideal here. It helps build the pot for when we hit our flush, folds out missed overcards that might have decent equity against our King-high, and gives us fold equity if our opponent missed the flop entirely. A bet of around half the pot is a solid size.

The flop is 6s-Th-6h, which is relatively dry. I hold QJ, giving me two overcards. The opponent's continuation bet of 350 into a 650 pot is standard for a button open, and with 350 to call into a total pot of 1000, the pot odds of 2.85:1 are sufficient to justify continuing. I have backdoor straight potential and two overcards that could potentially hit. Folding is too passive against a standard button continuation bet, so I will call to see the turn.

I am out of position with Queen-high on a board of 6s-Th-6h-7h. My hand has no pairs, no flush draws, and very little equity against a range that bet into me on the flop. The turn card, the 7h, completes a potential flush draw and improves the board further, making it even less likely that my high cards are good. Checking is the only sensible move, as betting would be a bluff into a range that likely has me beat, and I have no reason to bloat the pot further without a made hand.

The turn brings the 7h, completing my flush with Kh 3h. Although the board is paired, introducing some full house possibilities, my King-high flush is an extremely strong hand here. Flashlight's check gives me the opportunity to bet for value, targeting weaker flushes, a Ten with a heart kicker, or players clinging to a straight. A bet of around half the pot will charge draws and get value from second-best hands without folding them out too easily.

I am facing a bet on a board of 6s-Th-6h-7h holding QJ offsuit. The turn action has completed a flush draw and brought more connectivity to the board. My hand has effectively no showdown value, as even a pair of Queens or Jacks would be losing to any ten or better. I have no flush draw, and while I have a gutshot draw with a 9, the probability of hitting it and having it be the best hand is low, especially since the opponent is representing strength by betting into me again. With no pair and a weak range, calling is a losing play. I must fold.

I am on the button with QJ suited (Qc Jc), which is a very strong starting hand in a 3-handed game. I want to raise to build the pot and take control of the hand. A standard 3x open to 300 is appropriate here.

K6o is a weak hand, particularly out of position in the Small Blind against a Button open. I am not incentivized to defend this hand and play a pot out of position against an aggressive opponent. Folding is the most disciplined and long-term profitable decision here.

I am in the big blind facing a raise from the button. My hand is 9h 2h, which is heavily dominated, hits very few flops well, and will be difficult to play out of position. The correct strategic move is to fold.

I am on the button with 3-4 offsuit, which is an extremely weak hand with very little equity. Even though calling the blinds is relatively cheap, playing weak, uncoordinated cards out of position or in a multi-way pot is a recipe for losing chips in the long run. Discipline is key in a 150-hand tournament, so I will fold.

With 9s 4s in the small blind, my hand is extremely weak. Playing a suited gapper out of position against the big blind is generally a losing strategy. The optimal play is to fold and conserve my stack for better opportunities.
Pocket Twos on the Button is a standard open. I will raise to 300, which is my typical sizing, to try and steal the blinds or see a flop to set-mine.

I am in the Small Blind with 73 offsuit, which is one of the weakest starting hands in poker. The player on the Button (Pro) has raised to 300. Calling with such a weak hand out of position is a losing play in the long run, and 3-betting as a bluff is unnecessary given the hand's lack of post-flop potential. I will fold and wait for a better spot.

Ad6s is a standard defending hand in the big blind against a button open. With 200 chips to call to win a pot of 450, the pot odds are favorable, and the hand has enough strength to see a flop and evaluate against the opponent's aggressive opening range.

The board is monotone (Js-5s-7s), and I hold the 6s, giving me a flush draw. As the big blind out of position, the standard play is to check to the pre-flop aggressor to control the pot size and see how they proceed. My hand has decent equity, so check-calling is a viable plan if the bet is reasonable.

The flop comes monotone spades (Js 5s 7s). I have pocket twos with the 2 of spades, giving me bottom pair and the lowest possible flush draw. This board connects well with the big blind's defending range, which contains many hands with a higher single spade or made flushes. Betting here with an extremely weak pair and the worst flush draw doesn't accomplish much, as I'll mostly get called by better hands or superior draws. Checking behind to see a free turn card is the safest and most standard play.

The turn card (4d) has given me a massive amount of equity. I now have an open-ended straight draw (any 3 or 8 completes the straight) to go along with my flush draw (6s). The opponent checked back the flop, suggesting they do not have a strong hand like a made flush or a high pair, which means my semi-bluff has a significant chance of winning the pot immediately, in addition to my strong outs if called. A bet here allows me to take control of the hand and build a pot for when I hit one of my many outs.

My hand, pocket twos with the 2 of spades, is extremely weak on a Js 5s 7s 4d board. Flashlight's lead on the turn suggests they have connected with the board, have a made hand, or are semi-bluffing with a significantly better draw. Since I have an underpair to most of the board and the lowest possible flush draw, I have practically zero equity against their betting range. Calling or trying to bluff-raise here would be an unnecessary risk. Folding is the only sensible and profitable option.

I am on the button with Qs 8d in a three-handed game. This is a standard opening hand in this position to put pressure on the blinds and leverage my chip lead and positional advantage. I have consistently raised to 300 in previous hands and will maintain that sizing to keep my range balanced.

Pocket sevens are a strong, playable hand in this position. 3-betting from the small blind against the button's open raise is an effective way to isolate the aggressor, take the initiative, and potentially win the pot pre-flop or build value with a made hand. A raise to 1000 is a standard sizing to discourage a cheap call and define the button's hand range.

I am facing a 3-bet from the Small Blind after a Button open. I hold K7s in the Big Blind, which is too weak to call a 3-bet or 4-bet bluff, especially being out of position and with the original raiser still to act. Folding is the best choice.

I raised Q8o from the button, which is a standard steal attempt in a 3-handed game. However, Flashlight (SB) has responded with a 3-bet to 1000. Q8o is too weak to defend against a 3-bet, even with position. A bet of 950 into the 1400 pot is a solid value bet that is likely to get called by an Ace or even a stubborn King, given my check on the flop.

I hold a pair of Aces with a nine kicker. The board of K-A-4-5-7 is not heavily coordinated, and my opponent's check on the flop is a significant tell that they likely do not have a premium hand like AK or AQ that would have wanted to extract immediate value. The turn and river bets are consistent with a value bet from a weaker King, a lower pair turning into a bluff, or a missed draw. Given the pot size and the relative strength of top pair, calling is the most profitable play to avoid over-folding to potential bluffs or thin value bets.

I am on the button with Jack-Deuce offsuit. While I have been opening a wide range of hands from the button throughout the tournament to maintain aggression and steal blinds, J2o is near the bottom of the starting hand rankings. Pro has a relatively short stack (around 50BB) compared to the rest of the table and might be looking for a spot to defend or shove. There is no need to commit chips with such a weak hand when I have the chip lead.

With Flash on the button folding, I am in the Small Blind against the Big Blind. My hand, King-Six offsuit, is playable in a heads-up scenario from the small blind. I will raise to 300 to put pressure on the Big Blind, as this is a standard move to take down the blinds or play a controlled pot in position after the flop if called.

9h 2d is one of the weakest starting hands in Texas Hold'em. Facing a raise from the small blind, there is no justification to defend such a poor hand. Folding is the only optimal decision here to preserve my stack for better opportunities.

I am on the button with pocket deuces (2d, 2c). This is a strong starting hand that plays well in position. Since no action has occurred yet, raising to 300 is a standard opening move to either claim the blinds or see a flop with the betting lead. If I flop a set, I will have a disguised monster hand.

Facing a button raise from Flashlight, A4s is a marginal hand in the small blind. While it can be used as a 3-bet bluff, my 49bb stack and Flashlight's tendency to call 3-bets make playing a bloated pot out of position unappealing. Folding is a solid, low-variance choice to preserve my stack for better spots.

I am in the Big Blind with 8d 9s. Flashlight raised to 300 from the Button, and the Small Blind folded. I need to call 200 more into a pot of 450, giving me pot odds of 2.25:1. 89o is a reasonable hand to defend the Big Blind with against a Button open, as it has decent playability and I am closing the pre-flop action.

On a board of Jc 7c Ah, I have 9s 8d, which is a total miss. I am in the Big Blind and the pre-flop aggressor (Flashlight) is the Button. While I have a gutshot draw to a ten, the board favors the pre-flop raiser's range which includes many Aces and Jacks. Since I have no made hand and no strong draw, I will check and see how the opponent reacts, likely folding to any significant bet.

I am on the button with pocket deuces on a Jc-7c-Ah board. The Big Blind checked to me. My hand has very little showdown value and is not strong enough to bet for value against a calling range that likely includes many Aces and Jacks. While I have the deuce of clubs for a potential backdoor flush, it is too weak to justify a continuation bet. Checking back is the most prudent strategy to control the pot size and realize equity if I happen to improve on the turn.

On the turn card 5c, the board now shows three clubs. I still have no made hand with 9-high and my gutshot straight draw (needing a Ten) is weak. Flashlight checked back on the flop, which could indicate a weak hand or a desire to pot control. However, given the texture of the board (Ace-high with a flush possibility), I have no reason to lead out as a bluff without any relevant blockers or equity. I will check and hope to see a free river, but I am prepared to fold if a significant bet is made.

I am on the button with a flush draw (holding the 2c on a 3-club board). The opponent checked the turn, signaling potential weakness or a willingness to play a passive hand. Given my hand has very limited showdown value against an opponent who likely has at least an Ace or a pair, betting into them is risky and might not generate folds from better hands. Checking behind is the most disciplined approach, as it allows me to realize my equity for free if a club hits on the river while keeping the pot manageable.

I have 9-high on a board of Jc 7c Ah 5c 5d. I have no showdown value. The action went check-check on both the flop and the turn. While Flashlight's checks might indicate a weak hand, they still likely beat 9-high with any pair or even King-high. Leading out as a bluff after checking twice is rarely convincing and carries unnecessary risk for a small pot. I will check and hope for a miracle or simply concede the pot.

My pocket deuces (2d, 2c) have no showdown value on this Jc-7c-Ah-5c-5d board. The board itself plays as a pair of fives, and my hand is essentially air. I do not have a flush (I have four clubs total). Betting would be a pure bluff that is unlikely to succeed, as the opponent likely has at least a Jack or an Ace given the check-down. Checking behind is the only rational play to minimize risks.
K8s is a solid suited king on the Button. It is strong enough to open-raise to steal the blinds or play a pot in position. I'll stick to a standard open size of 3 big blinds.

I have been dealt the best starting hand in Texas Hold'em, pocket Aces. The Button (Pro) has opened for 300. I am in the Small Blind. With a stack of 12750 and Pro having a relatively short stack of 4600, my goal is to maximize value and potentially get Pro to commit their entire stack. A 3-bet here is standard to build the pot and protect my hand. I will raise to 1000, which is a standard sizing that invites a call or a shove from the original raiser while also putting pressure on the Big Blind.

I am in the Big Blind with King-Jack suited. Facing an open from the Button (Pro) and a 3-bet from the Small Blind (Flash), I have a hand that plays reasonably well post-flop, though I will be out of position. Given my deep stack and the strength of KJs, folding is too tight. I don't see a strong reason to 4-bet and bloat the pot out of position against a tight or aggressive range, so calling is the most balanced approach to see a flop.

I opened K8s from the button, but the Small Blind 3-bet and the Big Blind cold-called the 1000 chips. A cold-call of a 3-bet from the Big Blind is typically incredibly strong (often medium-to-large pocket pairs or premium broadways), and the Small Blind's 3-betting range is also very strong. My K8s is heavily dominated by both of their ranges (like AK, KQ, or big pocket pairs). Investing another 700 chips here—about 15% of my remaining stack—would be a major mistake. Folding is the safest and most optimal choice.

I have pocket Aces (Ad As) on a flop of 8c Qc 7s. The pot is 2300 after a pre-flop 3-bet which was called by the Big Blind (Flashlight). This board is somewhat coordinated with straight and flush draws, but my Aces are currently way ahead of most of Flashlight's range. I want to build the pot and get value from hands like KQ, QJ, QT, or flush draws. A bet of around 40-50% of the pot is appropriate here to keep worse hands in while charging draws. I will bet 1150.

I have a strong flush draw (King-high, second nuts) and overcards to the board on a flop of 8c-Qc-7s. The pot is offering me decent odds to call (1150 to win 4600). Given my stack depth and the strength of my draw, I have significant equity. Raising is an option to exert pressure, but calling allows me to realize my equity and keep the pot manageable against an aggressive opponent like Flash, while maintaining the ability to potentially bluff on later streets if I pick up more equity or if the opponent slows down.

The turn card is the Queen of Spades, which pairs the board. While I still have pocket Aces, the board pairing the top card is significant. If Flashlight has a Queen in their range (like KQ, QJ, or QT), they now have trips. However, they could also be holding a flush draw, a straight draw (like 9-10 or J-10), or a middle pocket pair (99, TT, JJ). By checking here, I am exercising pot control and protecting my range. If Flashlight checks back, I can safely evaluate the river. If they bet, I can determine based on the sizing whether they are likely representing a Queen or semi-bluffing with a draw. Checking also allows me to induce bluffs from missed draws on the river.

The opponent checked to me on the turn after leading the flop, which often indicates weakness or a hand that has lost showdown value. I hold the second nut flush draw, which gives me significant equity even if I am currently behind. By betting here, I can take down the pot immediately, or build the pot to increase my implied odds when I hit my flush on the river. A bet of roughly 1750 into the 4600 pot is a solid size to apply pressure without overcommitting, while setting up a potential river shove if I miss or hit.

I have pocket Aces, and the turn has paired the Queen. After I checked to control the pot, Flashlight bet 1750 into a pot of 4600. This bet sizing (around 38% pot) could represent a Queen (trips), but it could also be a semi-bluff with a flush or straight draw, or even a medium pair like JJ or TT testing where they stand. Given the pot odds (calling 1750 to win a total pot of 8100), folding is not an option here. I will call to see the river, keeping Flashlight's bluffs in their range and allowing myself to evaluate the final board state. Raising would likely only get called by hands that beat me (like a Queen or a full house).

I am in a strong position with pocket Aces, although the board has paired Queens. On the turn, I checked to control the pot and evaluate Flashlight's reaction, eventually calling their bet. The river card (2 of diamonds) is a complete brick that doesn't change the board texture. Flashlight's range includes some Queens (AQ, KQ, QJ) which now have trips, but it also includes many missed draws like club flush draws or straight draws (9-10, J-10, 6-5). By checking here, I am inducing Flashlight to bluff with their missed draws. If I bet, I likely only get called by hands that beat me (trips or better) while forcing out their bluffs. Checking allows me to reach a showdown or catch a final bluff from a wide range of hands I currently beat.

I missed my flush draw and am left with King-high. Flash checked to me on the river after calling my turn bet, which suggests they likely have some showdown value, such as a pocket pair or a middle pair like 7x or 8x. Since my King-high is very weak and will lose to any pair, I cannot reasonably expect to win by betting as a bluff given the opponent's willingness to call the turn. Checking behind is the most prudent play to reach showdown and minimize any further loss.

Holding JTo on the Button in a three-handed game is a clear opening hand. I have a significant chip lead over my opponents and want to maintain aggression. A standard 3x raise to 300 is consistent with the table's previous play and allows me to take control of the pot while having the advantage of position for the rest of the hand.

With JsTc, I have a playable hand in the small blind against a button open. While being out of position is a disadvantage, this hand has good connectivity and playability, making a call reasonable to see a flop. 3-betting is an option, but keeping the pot size manageable is safer given the stack depths and the fact that I will be out of position post-flop.

I hold 3s 2s in the Big Blind, which is a very weak and easily dominated hand. Facing a pre-flop raise from the Button and a call from the Small Blind, the pot odds might seem tempting, but the reverse implied odds are terrible since my flushes and straights will often be on the low end. Folding is the safest and most standard play to preserve my 45 BB stack.

I have middle pair with a Ten kicker on a board that contains a higher card (Queen) and potential flush/straight draws. Since I am out of position and checking to the pre-flop aggressor, checking is the most prudent strategy. This allows me to control the pot size and evaluate the strength of their continuation bet before committing more chips.

On this flop, I have middle pair (Jacks) with a Ten kicker. While the Queen is an overcard and there are straight and flush draws present, checking back might give a free card to a drawing hand. In Hand #6, I successfully bet a similar middle pair (J10 on a K-J-7 board) and Flashlight folded. I will place a standard continuation bet of 350 (half the pot) to take the initiative and see where I stand. If Flashlight calls, I will look to control the pot size on the turn unless the board improves my hand.

I have middle pair (Jacks) on a board of Jh-Qh-2d. The opponent has made a standard continuation bet of 350 into a pot of 700. Given that I have a pair, folding is too tight, and raising is risky against the button's opening range, which could include Queens, overpairs, or strong draws. Calling allows me to keep the pot size manageable and see a turn card, where I can re-evaluate my position.

I am out of position on the turn with a pair of Jacks and a gutshot straight draw (needing a King). The Ace on the turn is a significant card that likely improves the pre-flop aggressor's range significantly. Betting would likely be ineffective and put me in a tough spot if raised, so checking is the most prudent strategy to control the pot size and re-evaluate my position on the river.

The Ace on the turn is a significant scare card. While I had middle pair on the flop, I now effectively have third pair (behind Aces and Queens). Flashlight called the flop bet, suggesting they have a piece of the board or a draw. Checking back here is the safest play to control the size of the pot and reach showdown cheaply, as betting is unlikely to fold out a better hand like a Queen or an Ace, and I still have some showdown value against missed draws.

I have successfully made a Broadway straight (10-J-Q-K-A) on the river, which is the absolute nuts on this board. As the player in the small blind acting first, I need to bet to extract value from my opponent, Flash. A bet of 900 into the 1400 pot is a solid size that should encourage a call from a wide range of hands like top pair or two pair, while still being large enough to maximize my profit.

I have the Broadway straight (10-J-Q-K-A), which is the absolute nuts on this board because there are no pairs (no full houses/quads) and only two hearts (no flushes). Flashlight led for 900 on the river after check-calling the flop and checking the turn, which strongly suggests they have improved to a strong value hand like two pair, a set, or perhaps their own straight. Since I hold the nuts, I must raise for value. A raise to 2500 is a significant increase that targets their likely two-pair or set holdings while remaining at a call-able price point given the pot size.

I have the absolute nuts with a Broadway straight (T-J-Q-K-A). My opponent, Flash, has raised my value bet on the river, indicating significant strength and a willingness to commit chips. Since I hold the best possible hand, my goal is to extract the maximum amount of value. Shoving all-in is the optimal play to maximize potential winnings against his range, which likely includes strong holdings like sets, two-pair combinations, or possibly a lower straight that he believes is good.

I have the absolute nuts with the Broadway straight (10-J-Q-K-A). There are no pairs on the board, so a full house or quads is impossible, and there are only two hearts, so a flush is impossible. Flashlight's river 3-bet (raising my 2500 to 6850) signifies extreme strength, likely a lower straight or a set. Since Flashlight has 900 chips remaining in their stack, I will raise to 7750 to put them all-in and maximize my value from this unbeatable hand.

I hold the absolute nuts with a Broadway straight (T-J-Q-K-A). My opponent has re-raised me all-in on the river. Given that I have the best possible hand, calling the remaining 900 chips is mandatory to secure the pot.

I am on the button with King-Five offsuit. While this is a marginal hand, in a three-handed game, the button has a significant positional advantage. I will open with a standard raise of 300 to attempt to steal the blinds and maintain the aggressive image I have been cultivating.
I am in the Small Blind with 75o, facing a raise from the Button. This hand is too weak to play, especially out of position, so I will easily fold.

I am holding Jack-Four offsuit in the Big Blind. While the pot odds are roughly 2.25 to 1 to call the Button's raise, this hand has very poor playability and realization of equity out of position. Since I currently hold a significant chip lead and just came off a massive pot in the previous hand, there is no need to defend with such a weak range. I will fold and wait for a more advantageous situation.

I am on the button with Ace-Queen offsuit (AQo), which is a premium starting hand. Raising to 3 big blinds is the standard play here to either steal the blinds or build the pot and play in position with a strong hand against any callers.

In this 3-handed situation, Pro (the short stack with 41.5 BB) raises to 300 from the button. K9o is a decent hand but plays poorly out of position in the small blind against a wide button opening range. While I have the chip lead and could defend or 3-bet to pressure Pro, I choose to play conservatively and fold this marginal hand to avoid unnecessary volatility against the short stack, especially with the big blind (Flashlight) still to act and likely to defend or squeeze.

I am in the Big Blind with Jack-Deuce offsuit, a very weak holding. While the pot odds offer a decent price to call (200 to win a total pot of 650), J2o is dominated by almost any reasonable opening range from the button and will play poorly out of position post-flop. Continuing with this hand would likely lead to difficult decisions and negative expectation. The disciplined play is to fold and wait for a better spot.

Holding 8-2 offsuit on the button, this is one of the weakest possible hands in Texas Hold'em. While I have a significant chip lead and 3-handed play allows for wider opening ranges, 8-2o lacks any meaningful post-flop playability or high-card value. Flashlight has shown aggression in previous hands, and Pro is at a stack size where they might look to 3-bet shove. Folding is the most disciplined and standard play here.

With the Button having folded, I am heads-up against the Big Blind in the Small Blind position. Ace-Eight offsuit is a strong enough hand in this scenario to take the initiative. Raising to 300 allows me to exert pressure on the opponent's stack and potentially win the pot pre-flop. This sizing is consistent with my opening strategy throughout the tournament.

I am dealt 32 offsuit, which is arguably the worst starting hand in Texas Hold'em. Facing a 3x raise from the Small Blind, calling or 3-betting would be an unnecessary risk with a hand that has practically no equity or playability. Folding is the only sensible option to preserve my stack for better spots.

I am holding 9s-4d, which is a very weak starting hand. Even though I am on the button, there is no strategic reason to invest chips with such a poor hand. Folding is the most disciplined and prudent decision.

With A4o in the small blind and the button folding, this is a clear opportunity to steal the big blind. A4o blocks strong Ax hands and has decent high-card equity, making a standard raise to 3x (300) the most profitable play to win the pot uncontested or play post-flop with initiative.

Pro has demonstrated a very high frequency of opening and 3-betting with marginal or trash hands (like 52o, 24o, and 64o). While I have position on him post-flop and the price to call is attractive (2:1), 57 offsuit is simply too weak to reliably contest a pot, especially against a player who will likely apply heavy post-flop pressure. I will fold and wait for a better hand to exploit his wide range.
I am on the button with an extremely weak hand, 9d 4h (Nine-Four offsuit). It has no suitedness, no connectedness, and very low high-card value. This is a standard pre-flop fold from any position.

In a blind vs blind situation after the button folds, J5s is a strong enough hand to open for a raise. I will raise to 300 (a 3x open) to put pressure on the Big Blind and potentially win the blinds and antes immediately, while still having a hand with decent equity and playability if called.

I am in the Big Blind with Jack-Six offsuit against a raise from the Small Blind (Flash). While the hand is not premium, the pot odds are 2:1 (calling 200 to win a pot of 400), and I have position post-flop. Flash is an aggressive player, making it standard to defend wide in the Big Blind, especially when closing the action pre-flop.

On an Ace-high board like this (2s-Ac-6h), I have a significant range advantage as the pre-flop aggressor in a blind vs blind battle. While I only have Jack-high with a backdoor flush draw, this is a prime opportunity for a continuation bet. Flashlight's history suggests they are willing to peel one street with weak hands but often fold to further pressure if they haven't connected strongly. I will bet 300 (half pot) to represent the Ace and try to take the pot down immediately.

I have bottom pair (sixes) on a board of Ace-high. The pot offers me 3:1 odds to call the 300-chip bet. Given the opponent's aggressive nature established in previous hands, they are likely continuation betting with a wide range, including air, weak pairs, or an Ace. Folding would be too exploitive, and raising would likely fold out the bluffs I want to keep in the hand. Calling allows me to realize my equity and re-evaluate on the turn.

On the turn 5d, I have improved to third pair. Since an Ace is a significant part of the Big Blind's calling range after they called my pre-flop raise and flop bet, I will check to control the pot size and realize my showdown value. This play allows me to see a river for free if Flashlight checks back, or potentially evaluate a call if they bet, as my hand now has enough strength to catch some of their bluffs or smaller pairs.

I am in the Big Blind with a pair of sixes on an Ace-high board. Flash, who was aggressive on the flop, has checked the turn. Checking behind is the most prudent action here because my hand has marginal showdown value. Betting would only isolate me against hands that have me beaten (any Ace or pocket pair), and checking allows me to realize my equity and reach a showdown cheaply.

On the river, the board is 2s-Ac-6h-5d-Qh. I have a pair of Fives. The turn went check-check after I c-bet the Ace-high flop and got called. This check-check on the turn suggests Flashlight likely doesn't have a strong Ace. However, they could still have a weak Ace, a Six, or now a Queen. My pair of Fives has some showdown value against missed draws (like 43) or a pair of Deuces. There is little value in betting as better hands are unlikely to fold and worse hands are unlikely to call. I will check and hope to reach showdown cheaply. If Flashlight bets, I will have to evaluate based on the bet size, but checking is the most prudent play to realize my equity.

I am in the Big Blind with a pair of sixes on a board of Ace-Queen-Six-Five-Deuce. Flash, who was aggressive on the flop, has checked the turn and the river. Given the lack of betting on the later streets, Flash likely has a missed draw or a very weak hand. Since my pair of sixes has some showdown value and I do not want to fold out the hands I beat, checking behind is the most prudent action. Betting would turn my hand into a bluff, which is unnecessary as I am likely ahead of Flash's missed drawing range and would only get called by better hands.

I am on the button with Ace-Six suited (Ac 6c), which is a strong starting hand in a 3-handed game. My stack is quite large compared to my opponents, especially Pro who is the shortest with 45BB. I will open for a standard 3x raise to 300 to exert pressure and build a pot with a hand that has good post-flop playability and high card value.

I am in the Small Blind with Jack-Seven offsuit. This is a weak, non-connected hand. Calling out of position against an aggressive button raiser (Flash) is a negative expected value move. I should fold and wait for a more favorable spot to enter the pot.

With K5o in the Big Blind facing a button raise, my hand is too weak to defend. K5 offsuit plays very poorly post-flop out of position and is frequently dominated by the button's opening range. Folding is the safest and most standard play to preserve my 45 BB stack.
Being on the button with an Ace in my hand, I have a strong enough opening range to raise. Opening to 300 is standard for this table to steal the blinds or take initiative in the hand with position.

KTo is a marginal hand, especially when playing out of position from the small blind. Calling a 3x raise from the button leaves us vulnerable to a squeeze from the big blind and sets us up to play a difficult post-flop game with reverse implied odds. Folding is the safest and most standard play here.

I am in the Big Blind with 7h6h, a strong suited connector. Flashlight (Button) raised to 300, and Pro folded. I need to call 200 more to see a flop in a pot that will be 650, giving me excellent pot odds. Although I will be out of position, 7h6h plays very well post-flop and has great implied odds if I hit a straight or flush. Given my significant chip lead, calling is the most standard and effective play to realize my equity without over-committing pre-flop.

On this flop of 5d-8s-Td, I have an open-ended straight draw with 7h6h (needing a 9 or a 4 to complete the straight). Although the board has two diamonds, which I do not hold, my draw is strong. As the Big Blind caller, the standard play is to check to the pre-flop aggressor, Flashlight, to gauge the strength of their hand and potentially see a free card or call a reasonably sized bet to realize my equity.

The flop [5d, 8s, Td] is a reasonably dry board that fits my opening range well as the pre-flop raiser. Although I only have Ace-high, I also hold a backdoor flush draw (3d). C-betting into the opponent who checked allows me to maintain initiative and potentially take down the pot if they missed the flop, which is likely given the high frequency of air in a big blind's calling range.

I have an open-ended straight draw (OESD) with 7h6h on a 5d-8s-Td board. Flashlight's bet of 350 into a 650 pot provides me with sufficient pot odds to continue, especially when considering the potential implied odds if I complete the straight on the turn or river. Calling is the most prudent action here to realize my equity without over-inflating the pot while still on a draw.

On the turn, the board has paired with a second Ten (5d-8s-Td-Ts). I still hold an open-ended straight draw with 7h6h. As I am first to act and out of position against the pre-flop aggressor, the most standard play is to check. This allows me to see if Flashlight will check behind, granting me a free card to hit my straight, or to evaluate the size of any potential bet they might make before deciding whether to continue.

The turn paired the ten, which is a card that often helps the Big Blind's calling range. Since I only have Ace-high and the opponent called my flop bet, they likely have a piece of this board or a draw. Betting again would be a pure bluff into a potentially made hand. Checking back allows me to maintain pot control and keeps the variance low, as I have limited equity to improve.

On the river, the board has completed as 5d-8s-Td-Ts-Ac. I have missed my open-ended straight draw and currently hold 7-high, which has no showdown value. The Ace on the river is a significant scare card. However, after the turn went check-check, leading out (donk betting) into the pre-flop aggressor often looks like a polarized attempt to steal the pot and might be met with a call from any Ace or even a mid-pair that Flashlight decided to pot-control. Given my substantial chip lead and the fact that I am out of position, the most prudent and disciplined play is to check and concede the pot if Flashlight bets, or hope for a check-back if they also missed. I will not attempt a high-risk bluff here.

I hold a pair of Aces (Ah in hand, Ac on board). Given the board has a pair of tens, my Ace-kicker hand has some showdown value. The opponent checked the river after checking the turn, which suggests they either have a marginal hand, a missed draw, or a trapping hand. Betting for value is thin, as most hands I beat (lower pairs) might fold, and hands that beat me (tens) will call. Checking behind to realize my equity and take the pot at showdown is the most effective strategy.
I am on the button in a 3-handed game with A3o. This is a standard opening hand from the button to steal the blinds or play a pot in position. I'll raise to my standard pre-flop size of 3 big blinds.

K5o is a weak hand to defend from the small blind against a button open, especially being out of position for the rest of the hand. Given the stack sizes and the 3x open, folding is the most prudent play to preserve the chip lead.

I am in the big blind facing a raise from the button. Jack-Six offsuit is a marginal hand, but given the pot odds (calling 200 to win a pot of 450), it is mathematically correct to defend my big blind against a wide button opening range. I will call to see a flop and assess the situation.

I have flopped trips with a Jack, which is an extremely strong hand. Since I am the first to act on the flop as the Big Blind, checking is the optimal play to induce a continuation bet from the pre-flop aggressor, Pro. This allows me to trap them and extract value as the hand progresses, rather than betting out and potentially forcing them to fold their weaker hands.

With Ace high on a paired board, a small continuation bet is a standard play to fold out the opponent's equity and win the pot immediately. The board texture of 5c Jc Js is heavily weighted toward my range as the pre-flop raiser. Betting 200 puts pressure on my opponent's unmade hands while risking a minimal amount of chips.

I have flopped trips with Jacks, which is a dominating hand on this board. Pro has bet 200 into a 650 pot, which is a small continuation bet. I should call here to keep Pro's potential bluffs or lower pairs in the hand, rather than raising and forcing them to fold immediately. This allows me to continue the trap and extract more value on later streets.

I have flopped a full house, Jacks full of Fives. This is an incredibly strong hand, effectively the nuts, as any 5 in the opponent's hand would only make Fives full of Jacks, which is inferior to my Jacks full of Fives. Given the pot size of 1000 and the dry nature of the board, I need to start building the pot to maximize value. Leading out with a bet is the best way to ensure I extract chips from the opponent, who may have an Ace, a smaller pocket pair, or be floating. A bet of 600 is a solid value-oriented sizing that keeps the pot growing without being overly aggressive.

The turn double pairs the board (JJ55). Flashlight leading out for 600 indicates strength, representing at least a 5, a Jack, or a medium pocket pair (like 66-TT, which would make a better two pair than the board's 5s). Since I only hold Ace-high and have no strong draws to improve, calling or raising here is too risky given my remaining stack. Folding is the safest and most optimal play.

I am on the button in a 3-handed game, which is the most advantageous position. My hand, Q5o, is a marginal but standard open in this spot to apply pressure to the blinds. I have consistently used a 3x opening size (300 chips) throughout the tournament, and both Flashlight and Pro have shown a willingness to fold to pre-flop steals. Pro is relatively short-stacked at 38.5 BBs, so I must be wary of a potential shove, but Q5o is strong enough to attempt the steal.

I am in the small blind with Ace-Three offsuit against a button open from Flash. While Ace-Three is not a premium hand, it is strong enough to defend, especially with an Ace blocker. Playing out of position is a drawback, but calling allows me to see a flop and evaluate the texture of the board while keeping the pot manageable given my stack size of 10,350.

My hand is currently weak, so checking is the standard play to evaluate the opponent's action and control the size of the pot.

I have hit bottom pair (Fives) on a board of Js-8d-5d. As the pre-flop aggressor in position, a continuation bet is standard here. While my hand is marginal, betting around half the pot (350 chips) allows me to protect against overcards, extract value from draws (like diamond or straight draws), and maintain the initiative. Flashlight's check is a standard response after calling from the small blind, and this bet helps define their range while potentially ending the hand immediately if they missed completely.

The opponent (Flash) has shown a high frequency of continuation betting. The board (Js-8d-5d) is relatively dry, although it does offer some straight and flush draw possibilities. Given the pot odds (getting roughly 3:1 on the call) and my opponent's aggressive nature, folding an Ace-high hand here would be premature. Calling 350 allows me to see the turn card and re-evaluate, keeping my range wider and preventing the opponent from running over the table with small bluffs.

The turn has brought the 6d, placing three diamonds on the board (Js-8d-5d-6d). My Ace-high hand (Ah-3s) has no flush equity and is vulnerable. As I am out of position against an aggressive opponent who continuation-bet the flop, checking is the most prudent strategy. This allows me to control the pot size and re-evaluate my options based on the opponent's reaction without committing further chips to a hand with minimal showdown value.

Checking back is the safest play here to control the pot size, avoid a potential check-raise, and see the river for free to re-evaluate.

The board is heavily coordinated with four diamonds and cards that hit broadway ranges, specifically Queens and Jacks. My Ace-high hand has no diamond to participate in the potential flush and lacks any pair, making it extremely weak at showdown. Since the opponent (Flash) has checked both the turn and the river, they are likely signaling weakness or a hand they are hesitant to bet. Given the volatility of the board and my lack of showdown value, betting would be an unnecessary risk. Checking allows me to reach showdown without further committing chips to a likely losing hand, which is the most disciplined approach.

The board has completed with four diamonds (8d, 5d, 6d, Qd). While I have improved to two pair (Queens and Fives) on the river, the presence of four diamonds makes my hand extremely vulnerable. Any single diamond in Flashlight's hand beats me with a flush. Flashlight has checked twice on this coordinated board, which could indicate they are either trapping with a flush or, more likely, have a marginal hand like a Jack or an Eight and are afraid of the flush themselves. There is very little value in betting here; a hand worse than two pair is unlikely to call on a four-flush board, and I would be forced to fold to a check-raise. Checking back allows me to reach showdown and win against non-diamond hands without further risk.
Holding Ace-Ten suited on the button is a strong starting hand in a three-handed game. I should take the initiative by opening the betting to build the pot and put pressure on the blinds.

With Q8 suited in the Small Blind facing a 2.5x raise from the Button, I have a marginal hand that will play out of position if called. Calling would leave me vulnerable to a squeeze from the Big Blind and domination post-flop. I am in the Big Blind with Queen-Nine offsuit (Q9o). The Button (Flashlight) raised to 2.5x (250). Pro folded from the Small Blind. I need to call 150 more to see a flop. I have flopped top and bottom two pair on a very wet and coordinated board (Qh Jh 9s). The flop is Q-J-9, and I hold Ace-Ten of spades. This gives me the nut flush draw and a gutshot straight draw (needing a King). The pot is 550, and I am in position. A continuation bet is a strong play here to capitalize on my significant equity and fold equity, putting pressure on my opponent while setting up to potentially hit my strong draws.

I have flopped top and bottom two pair (Queens and Nines) on a very coordinated board (Qh Jh 9s). This board contains numerous straight draws (KT, T8, T9) and flush draws that likely connect with a Button opening range. While my hand is currently strong, it is highly vulnerable to any Ten, Jack, King, or Heart on the turn. I will check-raise Flashlight's continuation bet to 1100 to extract maximum value from weaker Queens, Jacks, and draws, while also protecting my equity by charging those draws a high price to continue.

The opponent check-raised significantly on a coordinated board of Q-J-9. While I do not have a flush draw (the board has hearts and I hold spades), I do have a gutshot straight draw with an Ace kicker. Given my position on the button, calling the raise to see a turn card is a standard play that balances pot odds with implied odds, allowing me to re-evaluate if I hit one of my outs or if the opponent slows down.

The turn 5h is a very significant card as it completes the heart flush draw that was present on the flop. While I still have two pair (Queens and Nines), the board is now incredibly coordinated with both straights (KT, T8) and flushes possible. When Flashlight called my flop check-raise, their range was narrowed down to strong Queens, Jacks with a heart, or draws. Since the flush hit, checking is the most prudent action. This allows me to control the size of the pot and evaluate Flashlight's response. If they check back, I can likely extract value on non-heart rivers; if they bet, I will have to decide if my two pair still has enough equity against their value range and potential bluffs.

The turn card completed the heart flush draw on a very coordinated board of Qh-Jh-9s-5h. Holding As-Ts, I do not have a flush, but I have the Ace of spades as a blocker and a gutshot straight draw (need a King). Flash has checked, which could be a trap or simply a sign of weakness. With the board texture being dangerous and my current hand having only marginal showdown value but decent equity if I hit my straight, checking behind is the optimal play to take a free river card and keep the pot size manageable, avoiding a potentially disastrous check-raise if I were to bet.

The river is the King of Hearts, which is one of the worst possible cards for my hand. The board now shows four hearts, meaning any single heart in Flashlight's hand makes a flush. Additionally, the King completes a straight for any Ten (like KT or T8), and since the King also pairs the board's existing straight draws, my two pair (Queens and Nines) has plummeted in relative value. There is no value in betting here; I will only be called by better hands (flushes, straights, or higher two pairs like KQ/KJ) and will likely fold out only hands I already beat. By checking, I can attempt to get to a cheap showdown if Flashlight checks back with a marginal hand or a missed non-heart draw. If Flashlight bets, I will likely have to fold given the high probability they hold a heart or a Ten.

I have flopped an Ace-high straight on the river with As Ts, as the King of Hearts completed the board. However, the board is extremely dangerous with four hearts present. Any single heart in my opponent's hand results in a flush, which beats my straight. Since the opponent checked, betting for value is risky as I will likely only get called by better hands (flushes), and worse hands will fold. Checking behind is the most prudent play to see the showdown and keep the pot controlled.
75o is a very weak hand, especially with 38 BBs. Folding from the button is the standard and correct play.

In a blind-vs-blind situation after the button folds, 8-7 offsuit is a reasonable hand to attempt a steal or play a small pot. I will raise to 300 to put pressure on Flashlight in the Big Blind. While I will be out of position post-flop, the hand has decent connectivity if called.

Flash has been hyper-aggressive, opening almost every hand from the small blind. With Ace-Deuce offsuit, I have a decent hand heads-up that has significant showdown value against a wide range. The flop of 3d-6c-3s is a paired, dry board which generally favors the pre-flop aggressor. The board is very dry (3-3-6), which favors neither player significantly, but makes it highly unlikely that either has a strong hand (like a trey). Flash is playing aggressively and likely making a standard continuation bet. My Ace-high hand has solid showdown value against their wide, aggressive opening range. The turn card is the King of Clubs, which is a scare card that fits my perceived range as the pre-flop aggressor. The opponent has checked the turn on a paired board (3-3-6-K). My hand is just Ace-high. Betting would likely force the opponent to fold their worst air but call with anything that beats me (like a pair of sixes, any King, or better). Since I have no equity and a bluff is unlikely to succeed against a player who has already shown a willingness to see the hand through, I will check and concede the pot if Flashlight has any pair or better.

The opponent has checked the river, and I hold only Ace-high. Betting would likely force a fold from air but get called by any pair, which beats me. Checking back is the most prudent way to realize whatever showdown value my Ace-high has against the opponent's range while avoiding unnecessary risks in a pot where I am unlikely to have the best hand.

I am on the button with 3d 4h, which is a very weak hand. Given my stack size and the position, there is no strategic reason to enter the pot with such a low-equity hand. Folding is the most prudent move to preserve my chips.

I am in the Button position with a very weak hand (Ten-Four offsuit). Raising or calling with this hand is negative expected value, as it does not play well post-flop and provides no incentive to enter the pot. Folding is the standard and correct strategic choice.
