Calling is a solid play that allows me to see a flop, realize my hand's equity, and keep the pot manageable while playing out of position.

I am in the Big Blind with Ace-Deuce suited (Ad2d). Flashlight raised from the button to 300, and Pro called from the Small Blind. I need to call 200 to see the flop in a pot that will be 900. These are excellent pot odds (4.5 to 1). My hand plays well post-flop in multi-way pots, and since I am closing the action, calling is a very standard and profitable play. Raising is an option to thin the field, but given the short stack of Pro (Small Blind), I'd rather not induce a shove and instead see a flop cheaply.

I flopped top pair with a Jack kicker on a dry, paired board. Being out of position in a 3-way pot, checking to the pre-flop raiser (Flashlight) is the standard and most balanced play. Leading out here could isolate me against stronger hands like AQ, KQ, or trips, while causing weaker hands to fold. By checking, I control the size of the pot, keep weaker hands in, and give the pre-flop raiser a chance to continuation bet, which I can then call.

On a board of 6s-Qc-6d, I have Ace-high with Ad2d. This board is relatively static. Pro has checked from the Small Blind. As I am in the Big Blind and the pre-flop raiser (Flashlight) is still to act behind me, the most standard play is to check. Betting here into two opponents on a paired board with only Ace-high is unlikely to fold out better hands like a Queen or a Six, and I have some marginal showdown value. I will check and see how Flashlight responds to the checked action.

I have flopped trip sixes on a dry, paired board, which is a very strong hand. Since both opponents checked to me on the button, I should lead with a bet to start building the pot and extract value from any Queens or pocket pairs in their ranges.

I have top pair with a good kicker on a dry, paired board. Flashlight's continuation bet of half the pot could easily be a standard c-bet with a wide range of hands, including overcards, smaller pocket pairs, and pure bluffs. My hand is strong enough to continue, but not strong enough to check-raise, as a raise would likely fold out all his bluffs and only get called or shoved on by better hands like AQ, KQ, or overpairs. Calling controls the pot size and allows me to keep his weaker hands in the pot while re-evaluating on the turn.

I have Ace-high (Ad2d) on a 6s-Qc-6d flop. Flashlight (the pre-flop aggressor) has bet 450 into two opponents, and Pro (Small Blind) has called. Although the pot odds are attractive (calling 450 to win a total pot of 2250, or 5:1), the fact that Pro called ahead of me significantly decreases the chance that my Ace-high is currently the best hand or that my Ace outs are clean. Pro is short-stacked and his call likely indicates a Queen, a Six, or a pocket pair. Without a real draw and facing action from two players, I should not commit more chips with just Ace-high.

I have top pair with a Jack kicker on a paired board where a flush draw has now developed. The standard line after check-calling the flop is to check the turn to the pre-flop aggressor. Leading out (donk betting) here would likely only be called or raised by better hands like AQ, KQ, or overpairs, while folding out all of Flashlight's bluffs and weaker made hands. By checking, I maintain pot control, induce potential bluffs from his missed overcards or newly acquired draws, and allow him to check back his marginal hands.

I have trip sixes on a relatively dry board (6-Q-6-4). Pro checked to me again on the turn. I should continue to value bet to build the pot against a Queen or an underpair. A bet of 900 into the 1800 pot is a standard value size to keep building the pot.

I have top pair with a Jack kicker on a relatively dry, paired board. Flashlight's double-barrel on the turn could be a value bet with a better Queen or an overpair, but it could also easily be a semi-bluff with a newly acquired spade flush draw, or just continued aggression with overcards. Folding top pair here to a half-pot bet is too weak against a button opener's wide range. However, raising all-in would only get called by better hands like AQ, KQ, AA, KK, or a 6, while folding out his bluffs. Calling is the best option to keep his bluffs in the pot and re-evaluate on the river.

I have top pair with a Jack kicker on a board where the backdoor flush draw has completed. Being out of position with a marginal made hand and a stack of 1800 into a pot of 3600, leading out here would only isolate me against better hands like flushes, better Queens (AQ, KQ), or trips, while folding out all of Flashlight's bluffs or weaker pairs. By checking, I maintain pot control, allow Flashlight to bluff with any missed straight draws, or check behind with his marginal value hands.

I have improved to a full house (sixes full of sevens) on the river, which is a very strong hand. Pro checked to me after calling the turn, indicating they likely have a Queen or a made hand they are not willing to bet themselves. With a pot of 3600, I will place a value bet of 1800 to extract additional chips from their range.

Facing a triple barrel from the pre-flop aggressor on a board where the flush draw has completed, my top pair with a Jack kicker has been reduced to a mere bluff catcher. Flashlight's sizing on every street represents a strong range, heavily weighted towards value hands that crush me, such as flushes, trips, stronger Queens (AQ, KQ), and overpairs. Calling here would be for my tournament life with a very marginal holding. Folding preserves my remaining 18 BB stack, allowing me to find a better spot to commit my chips.

With Pocket Jacks (JJ) on the Button and an 18 BB stack, I have a premium starting hand. A standard 3x raise to 300 is the best play here to build the pot while keeping my opponents' calling ranges wide. If either blind 3-bets, I am fully prepared to get all my chips in.

I am in the Small Blind with Jack-Nine suited. The Button (Pro) has raised to 300. Pro is very short-stacked with only 1,500 total (15 BBs). Calling 250 more is a reasonable price for a hand with this much playability and potential to flop well. While 3-betting could isolate the short stack, J9s plays well enough to see a flop, and I want to keep the pot manageable against the Big Blind (Flashlight), who also has a large stack. If Pro shoves post-flop, I'll evaluate based on the board texture.

Jack-three offsuit is a very weak hand with poor playability, especially when facing a raise and a call in a three-way pot. The pot odds do not justify calling with such a marginal holding. Folding is the disciplined and correct strategic choice.

On this flop of 5d-3d-Qs, I have Jack-high with no direct draws or pairs. The Button (Pro) was the pre-flop aggressor and has a very short stack of 1,200 remaining. Since I am out of position and have completely missed the board, the most prudent play is to check and see how the Button responds. Given Pro's short stack, a bet from him often indicates he's committed to the hand or has a piece of the board, making a bluff-catch or a check-raise attempt risky with only Jack-high.

The flop comes 5d 3d Qs, presenting a single overcard to my Pocket Jacks alongside a flush draw. Flash checks to me. While the Queen is mildly concerning, my hand is likely still best against a wide small blind calling range. A continuation bet is necessary to extract value from flush draws, straight draws, and smaller pocket pairs (like TT-66), while also denying free equity to hands with overcards like AK or AJ. A bet of about 1/3 of the pot (250 chips) accomplishes this and allows me to maintain pot control given my relatively short stack.

I have Jack-high with no draws on a Queen-high board. The Button (Pro) has made a small continuation bet of 250 into a 700-chip pot. While the pot odds are technically favorable for a call, my hand has very little equity against even a standard bluffing range (like Ace-high or King-high) and virtually none against any pair. Pro is also extremely short-stacked, meaning any further commitment on the turn would likely involve him going all-in. Without a pair or a draw, there is no reason to continue in this hand and bleed chips. I will fold and wait for a more advantageous situation.

I am on the button with Queen of Clubs and Four of Diamonds. While the button is an ideal position to steal the blinds, Q4 offsuit is too weak to justify a raise, especially with the Big Blind (Pro) having a short stack of only 2100 chips. Pro is likely looking for a spot to shove, and I do not want to be forced to fold to a 3-bet or call off with a marginal hand. Additionally, Flashlight in the Small Blind has been quite active. I will wait for a better spot.

I am in the small blind and the button has folded. My opponent in the big blind is very short-stacked (2,100 chips), so I should raise to apply pressure. King-Ten offsuit is a strong enough hand to open-raise in this position against a short stack.

I am in the Big Blind with a weak offsuit hand, Ten-Three. Facing a raise to 300 from the Small Blind, this hand has very little equity and playability, especially given my short stack. The standard and optimal play is to simply fold.

7d 3d is a very weak starting hand, even on the button. With two opponents to face, including a short stack in the small blind, there is no strategic value in playing this hand. Folding is the standard professional decision to preserve stack and avoid unnecessary risk.

With Ad 2h in the Small Blind and the Button folding, I have a decent hand to steal the Big Blind. My stack is getting short at 20.5 BBs, but A2o is strong enough to open-raise here. I'll stick to my standard 3x raise to 300 to apply pressure.

With 4d 9c in the Big Blind, I am facing a 3x raise from Pro in the Small Blind. Pro is currently short-stacked with only 1800 chips. While the pot odds are 2:1, 4-9 offsuit is a very weak hand with poor playability and low equity against almost any semi-reasonable opening range. Given Pro's short stack, a call here often leads to a commitment on the flop or a fold to a continuation bet. It is more prudent to fold this trash hand and wait for a better spot to utilize my stack advantage.

With A6 suited on the Button and 22 BBs, I have a strong enough hand to open-raise. A min-raise or a 3x raise is standard; my previous opens have been 300 chips. Raising to 300 puts pressure on the blinds and allows me to play in position if called.

Pro is the short stack with roughly 22 BBs and has opened for 3x from the button. My hand, 8d 6h, is a weak offsuit connector that performs poorly out of position. Given Pro's short stack, his opening range is likely stronger than a typical button raise, and calling would also leave me vulnerable to a squeeze from the chip leader in the big blind. Folding is the most prudent play to preserve my healthy stack.

I am in the big blind with Queen-Ten suited (Qd Td) and face a raise to 300 from the Button (Pro). The pot odds are favorable (calling 200 to win a total pot of 650), and Qd Td is a strong enough hand to defend against a standard open raise. Calling is the most disciplined play to maintain control and see a flop.

The flop of 9s Jc 8h gives me a double gutshot straight draw (needing a King or a 7) along with an overcard. Since I am out of position against a pre-flop aggressor (Pro), checking is the most standard and defensive line. It allows me to control the pot size while seeing how my opponent reacts to the flop, and I can evaluate my options for calling or folding if a bet comes, or perhaps check-raise if I decide to semi-bluff later.

The flop of 9s-Jc-8h is very coordinated and heavily favors the Big Blind's defending range, which includes many straight draws, pairs, and two-pairs. With just Ace-high and a backdoor flush draw, a continuation bet is likely to get called or raised, putting our relatively short stack at risk with very little equity. Checking behind to realize our equity and control the pot size is the most prudent decision.

The turn card (2h) is a blank that does not improve my hand, but I still hold a double-gutshot straight draw (needing a 7 or a King). Pro checked back the flop, which often indicates weakness or a decision to keep the pot small. Since I am out of position and have a drawing hand, checking is the most effective way to control the pot size, realize my equity, and keep my options open for the river.

Flashlight has checked again on the turn. Our Ace-high still has some showdown value, and the board remains very coordinated, favoring the Big Blind's calling range. Since we only have 1900 chips left and 650 in the pot, bluffing here with no clear draw (only an overcard) is risky. By checking, we control the pot size and get a free card to try and improve or evaluate the river.

The river card 5h did not improve my hand, leaving me with Queen-high, which has very little showdown value. However, Pro has checked down both the turn and the river, indicating a lack of a strong hand. A bluff here would be high-risk and unlikely to force a fold from any pair, and I cannot win by betting. Checking is the most logical and safe play to reach showdown.

Flashlight has checked all three streets, indicating marginal showdown value or a missed draw like QT or T7. My Ace-high has a reasonable chance of being the best hand at showdown. Betting here would essentially turn my hand into a bluff, and I would likely only be called by better hands (such as any pair or a slowly-played flush). Checking behind allows me to realize my showdown value for free and protects my short stack.

I am on the button with 9s 4d, which is a very weak starting hand. While I have the chip lead over the Big Blind (Pro), who is very short-stacked at 18 BB, 94o does not perform well enough to justify a steal attempt here. Pro is likely to shove any semi-decent hand to double up, and I don't want to commit chips to a pot where I'll likely have to fold to a 3-bet. Folding is the safest and most standard play.

I hold pocket Aces, the best starting hand. The Button has folded, leaving only the Big Blind (Pro) to act. Given the Pro is short-stacked with only 18 big blinds, I want to build the pot and potentially induce an all-in from them. Raising to 300 is a standard sizing to pressure the Big Blind and extract value.

With only 18 big blinds remaining (1800 chips behind), AJo is a premium hand in a blind-versus-blind scenario. The Small Blind's opening range is typically very wide in this spot. A 3-bet all-in maximizes fold equity against his marginal opening hands and prevents us from having to play out of position with an awkward stack-to-pot ratio if we miss the flop. If called, AJo still retains excellent equity. Shoving is the most profitable and standard play.

actions.message: There is no reason to complete, bet, or raise since every other player has either folded or gone all-in.

actions.thought: I am on the button with 9-10 offsuit. While this is not a premium hand, being the chip leader and in the button position, it is a reasonable hand to open the pot with to steal the blinds, especially given the short stack of Pro who might be desperate. A raise to 300 is the standard opening size observed in previous hands.

With J7o in the small blind facing a button raise, my hand is simply too weak to defend, especially considering my short stack. A fold is the standard and correct play here to preserve chips for a better spot.

I am in the Big Blind holding 3s 7s. Flashlight has raised to 300 from the button, and Pro has folded. Although I am getting decent pot odds (200 to call into a 450 pot), 3s 7s is a very weak hand to play out of position, especially against the chip leader. In a 3-handed game, I need to defend my blinds, but this hand is at the bottom of the range and doesn't flop well enough to justify the call while I have a healthy stack and a short-stacked opponent (Pro) is still in the game.

J7o is a weak, unconnected, unsuited hand that is not profitable to play, even from the button. With a relatively short stack of 21 big blinds, preserving chips for better starting hands or better shoving spots is essential.

I am in the Small Blind with Qs 5c. The Button has folded, leaving only me and the Big Blind (Flashlight). Flashlight is the chip leader and has been quite active, frequently defending their blind or 3-betting. Q5o is a marginal hand, but in a 3-handed game, it's often strong enough to attempt a steal from the Small Blind. I have consistently used a 3x open (300) throughout the match. I will raise to 300 here to try and take down the blinds; however, if Flashlight 3-bets, I will likely fold given the hand's weakness.

I am in the Big Blind with Queen of Hearts and Ten of Hearts, a strong suited connector. My opponent, Flash, has raised from the Small Blind. Since I have position post-flop as the Big Blind in a heads-up scenario, calling the raise is a solid, standard play to see a flop and realize my equity with a strong hand, especially given the stack sizes.

The flop is Ace-high (Ah 3c 4s), which is a very favorable board for the pre-flop raiser. Although I only have Queen-high, I need to continue representing a strong hand like an Ace or a high pocket pair. A half-pot continuation bet of 300 should be enough to force folds from many of Flashlight's marginal hands that missed this board entirely. If I am called or raised, I will likely give up on the hand as I have no real draw or showdown value.

The flop is Ah-3c-4s. I have Queen-Ten of hearts. My opponent, Flash, continuation-bet 300 into a 600 pot. While the bet is small, my hand is extremely weak here, only containing a backdoor flush draw and no pair or significant equity against an Ace, which is highly likely in the opponent's raising range. With 150 hands to play, I don't need to force a float in this spot. Folding is the disciplined, low-variance choice.

I am on the button with a very weak hand, Jack-Four offsuit. While I have a healthy stack of 12,300, the Big Blind (Pro) is extremely short-stacked with only 2,050 chips. If I raise to the standard 300, I invite a shove from the short stack, which I would be forced to fold to given my hand strength. There is no need to risk chips or provide the short stack an opportunity to double up with such a sub-optimal hand. Folding is the most disciplined and mathematically sound play here.

With the button folding, I am in the small blind against the short-stacked big blind (Pro, 2050 chips). My hand, King-Queen offsuit, is a strong holding in a heads-up situation, allowing me to comfortably put pressure on the short stack. Raising to 300 is an effective size to either take down the blinds immediately or force the short stack to make a difficult decision with their remaining chips.

With a 20.5 BB stack, defending K8o to a 3x raise from the small blind is risky. Calling commits 10% of my stack with a marginal offsuit hand, and shoving is too loose since K8o performs poorly against the calling range of the small blind. Folding preserves my short stack for a better spot.

Js7c is a marginal hand, but as the button, I have position and the opportunity to steal the blinds. Given my aggressive history and the need to accumulate chips, a standard open-raise is appropriate to apply pressure on the blinds.

Holding Pocket Aces in the Small Blind facing a 3BB open from the Button. With exactly 20BBs (2000 chips) remaining, a 3-bet shove is the most effective and standard play. It maximizes value against pairs and high-card hands (like AK, AQ, JJ-KK) that are likely to call a 20BB shove, prevents us from playing out of position with an awkward stack-to-pot ratio post-flop, and fully protects our premium equity.

I am in the Big Blind with 6d 4d. Flashlight opened for 300, and Pro has 3-bet to 2000, which is nearly all of their remaining chips. While my hand is suited, it is extremely weak and does not play well against a 3-bet range from a short-stacked player looking to double up. Additionally, the original raiser, Flashlight, still has to act and could easily 4-bet or call, leaving me squeezed in a large pot with a marginal hand. I will preserve my stack and fold.

Facing a 3-bet from the small blind after my initial open on the button, my hand (Js7c) is simply not strong enough to warrant a call or a 4-bet. Discarding the hand is the most disciplined and profitable long-term decision given the strength indicated by the opponent's 3-bet.

With King-Ten offsuit on the Button in a 3-handed game, I have a strong enough hand to open for a raise. My stack is around 24.5 big blinds, which gives me enough room to make a standard raise to 300 and fold to a 3-bet, or play positionally post-flop if called. Opening from the Button is standard here to steal the blinds and capitalize on our positional advantage.

I am in the Small Blind with 9-4 offsuit, which is an extremely weak hand. The Button (Pro) has raised to 300, and they are short-stacked with only 2150 chips. Defending with such a weak hand out of position against a short stack who is likely looking to commit is not profitable. Additionally, the Big Blind (Flashlight) is the chip leader and could easily squeeze. I will fold and wait for a better spot.

I am in the big blind with an Ace-high hand (Ad 5c) facing a standard raise from the button. The pot odds (200 to call into a total pot of 450) justify defending the blind here. A call is the most standard and profitable play to see a flop.

The flop is Jh-8h-2d. My hand (Ad-5c) is weak and offers little besides an overcard-like Ace and a backdoor flush draw. Betting into the button (who has the positional advantage) is not a high-percentage play as it turns my hand into a pure bluff. Checking is the most standard and effective way to proceed, keeping the pot size manageable and allowing me to re-evaluate based on the opponent's action.

The board is Jh 8h 2d. I hold Kc Th, missing the flop completely with only King-high and no direct straight or flush draws. This board texture connects quite well with the Big Blind's defending range, which includes many Jx, 8x, heart flush draws, and straight draws like T9 or 97. Given my relatively short stack and lack of equity, a continuation bet here is unlikely to generate enough folds to be profitable. Checking behind is the best option to see a free turn card, potentially pairing my King or picking up some equity, while keeping the pot small.

The turn has been checked through by both players. With only Ace-high (Ad-5c) on a board of Jh-8h-2d-4c, I have very little showdown value and limited equity to improve. Betting would turn my hand into a pure bluff, which is likely ineffective against a button who checked back the flop, potentially indicating a range that connects with this board or at least has some protection. Checking allows me to see the river for free and minimizes risk, keeping the pot small while maintaining a passive approach to a weak hand.

The turn is a brick (4c) and my opponent checks again. I still only have King-high with no strong draws. While a delayed continuation bet could fold out some better high-card hands or weak bottom pairs, my King-high actually has some showdown value against busted straight or flush draws. Keeping the pot small and seeing a free river card is the most prudent play, preserving my short stack for a better opportunity.

The river has been reached after both players checked the flop and turn on a board of Jh-8h-2d-4c-Qs. My hand, Ace-high (Ad-5c), has no showdown value against any pair. Checking is the only logical move as betting as a bluff is unlikely to force a fold from any hand that beats me (which is almost any pair), and I should minimize any risk of losing further chips. I will check to see the result.

The river brings a Qs and my opponent checks for the third time. My King-high actually has reasonable showdown value here against busted flush draws, straight draws like T9 or 97, and other weaker high-card combinations. Betting here would turn my hand into a bluff, risking a significant portion of my relatively short stack, and I'm unlikely to get better hands like Ace-high or small pairs to fold. Checking behind allows me to realize my showdown equity risk-free.

I am on the button with 7c 2s, which is the worst starting hand in poker. While I could attempt to steal the blinds, the Big Blind (Pro) is extremely short-stacked with only 2050 chips and is likely looking for any reasonably playable hand to shove over a raise. Given the tournament progress and my healthy stack, there is no reason to risk chips or get involved with a trash hand. I will fold and wait for a better spot.

With 7-8 offsuit in the small blind after the button has folded, completing the small blind to see a cheap flop against the big blind (Pro) is a standard play. Given Pro's short stack, there is no need to overcommit with a marginal hand pre-flop. I will call to match the big blind.

I hold Pocket Kings in the Big Blind and the Small Blind has just limped. I want to build the pot and extract maximum value from my premium hand. With a stack of about 20 big blinds, I will raise to 350 to encourage a call from worse hands while setting up to get the rest of my chips in on the flop.

Pro has raised to 350, significantly increasing the pot after I already completed my small blind. Holding 7-8 offsuit, I am out of position against a short-stacked opponent. While I initially completed the blind to see a cheap flop, calling the raise with such a marginal hand is not mathematically sound. Given the stack sizes and the positional disadvantage, folding is the most disciplined decision to preserve my stack.

Holding 2h-3h on the button in a 3-handed game is extremely weak. Even with position, there is very little equity and the hand plays poorly against almost any range. Folding is the standard, disciplined play to preserve my stack for a better opportunity.

I am in the Small Blind with 97o, a weak unsuited hand. Flashlight has folded on the button. With a short stack of 22 big blinds, I cannot afford to bleed chips by limping or raising with sub-par hands out of position against the Big Blind. Folding is the most straightforward and optimal decision here.
I am on the button with 83o, which is a very weak starting hand. With 22 BBs left, I need to look for much better spots to commit my chips. Folding is the only viable option here.

I am in the Small Blind with J4o. The Button (Pro) has folded. Flashlight is the chip leader in the Big Blind and has been playing aggressively. Jack-four offsuit is a very weak hand that does not play well post-flop, especially out of position against a big stack. Given the current stack sizes and the low quality of my hand, folding is the standard and most disciplined play.

I am on the button with Ace-Queen suited, a premium hand. With a stack of 121.5 big blinds, I want to open for a standard raise of 3x. The Big Blind (Pro) is short-stacked with only 21 big blinds, and I am happy to call if he decides to shove. Flashlight in the Small Blind has a large stack and has been active, so I want to build a pot while in position.

I am in the small blind with King-Ten offsuit. While KTo is a decent hand, playing it out of position against an aggressive button raiser like Flash can be tricky. My stack is healthy, and I prefer to see a flop without overcommitting to a 3-bet pot. Calling allows me to evaluate the flop and control the pot size while staying disciplined.

With K7o in the Big Blind facing a raise from the Button and a call from the Small Blind, the hand is too weak to play multi-way out of position. With a stack of 21 BBs, it's best to preserve chips and fold.

The flop is Ace-high, which heavily favors the pre-flop raiser's range. With King-Ten offsuit, I have minimal equity against an Ace, although I do have a backdoor spade draw. Since I am out of position, checking is the standard play to control the pot size and let the aggressor, Flash, dictate the action. If Flash checks back, I can re-evaluate on the turn; if Flash bets, I will assess the sizing before deciding whether to continue.

I have flopped top pair with the nut flush draw. This is an extremely strong position. Since Flashlight checked to me, I will place a standard continuation bet to build the pot for value and to protect against potential straight draws like 8-9 or 5-4. A bet of roughly 60% of the pot is appropriate here.

The flop is Ace-high, which heavily hits the button's opening range. While Flash's bet of 400 into 600 is a standard continuation bet, my King-high with only backdoor spade potential is insufficient to continue against a range that likely contains many Aces. Given that I am out of position, calling just to hope for a turn check-down or to hit a runner-runner is not profitable in the long run. I will fold and preserve my stack for a better spot.
K-Q offsuit is a strong hand in position. Raising is the standard play to take initiative and build the pot.

With a short stack of around 20 big blinds, playing a marginal suited gapper like J8s out of position against a button raise is unprofitable. Calling would commit too much of my stack out of position, and jamming is far too loose. The best decision is to fold and look for a better spot to commit my chips.

I am in the Big Blind with 8-4 offsuit. Flashlight, the chip leader, has raised to 300 from the button. Although I am getting decent pot odds to call (200 to win a total pot of 650), 8-4 offsuit is a very weak hand to play out of position against an aggressive chip leader. With 12,450 chips, I have a solid stack and do not need to defend with such a marginal hand. I will preserve my chips for a better spot.
With T6o on the button and a 20 big blind stack, this hand is simply too weak to play. I need to conserve my chips and wait for a better spot to push or raise.

I am in the Small Blind with Pocket Kings (Kh Ks), the second-best starting hand in Texas Hold'em. The Button (Pro) has folded, leaving only the Big Blind (Flashlight) to contend with. With a premium hand like this, I want to build a pot and extract value. My standard opening raise throughout this tournament has been to 300, so I will maintain that sizing to remain balanced while building the pot with my monster hand.

I am in the Big Blind with Ace-Three offsuit. Flash has raised to 300 from the Small Blind. Given the pot odds and my position, defending my blind is standard, especially since Ace-high has decent equity against an aggressive opponent's likely range in this spot.

I have an overpair (Pocket Kings) on a very dry paired board (9-9-2). This is a strong hand that is likely ahead of Flashlight's range. Since I was the pre-flop aggressor, I will follow up with a continuation bet to extract value from smaller pocket pairs, or from Ace-high hands that might call a single street. A half-pot bet of 300 is standard here to maintain pressure and build the pot.

The board is 9h-9d-2c, which is a very dry board. Flash has bet 300 into a 900 pot, giving me 4:1 pot odds. With Ace-high (Ad-3s), I have good blockers to his range if he is continuation betting with overcards or air. Given our history of aggressive play, calling here to see the turn is standard, as his range is wide from the Small Blind.

The turn card (5s) is essentially a brick that doesn't significantly alter the board texture. I still hold an overpair (Kings), which is likely the best hand unless the opponent has a Nine or a rare set of fives/deuces. Given that Flashlight called a pre-flop raise and a flop continuation bet, their range likely consists of mid-pocket pairs (66-QQ), Ace-high hands, or a Nine. To extract value from those pocket pairs and protect my hand against Ace-high draws, I will lead out with another value bet. A bet of 700 into a 1200 pot (roughly 58% pot) is a standard sizing to keep building the pot while remaining priced reasonably for a call from weaker hands.

The board is extremely dry (9-9-2-5) and my opponent, Flash, has barrelled on the turn for 700 into a 1200 pot. With only Ace-high (Ad-3s), I have no pair and no realistic draw. Calling here would require me to beat a wide range of bluffs or air, but given Flash's aggression on this specific texture, he is likely betting for value or protection, putting me behind in the hand. Since I have no showdown value and minimal equity, folding is the most disciplined play to preserve my stack.
